summary: I reviewed the changed auth paths, admin mutations, public display/player surfaces, logging, and schema/migration changes for exploitable security and data-integrity risks. The visible server-action auth checks and public column narrowing are good, but several protections are still implemented as non-atomic application checks. The highest-risk areas are permissive Supabase RLS and multi-step live-event mutations that can drift under races or partial failures.
appears_sound: Admin and host mutations re-fetch the Supabase user and profile role server-side via `authorizeAdmin` and `authorizeHost` before proceeding (`src/app/admin/actions.ts:17`, `src/app/host/actions.ts:16`).

Display and player routes use narrow public select lists against `game_states_public`, excluding host-only fields such as `number_sequence` and `controlling_host_id` (`src/app/display/[sessionId]/page.tsx:12`, `src/app/player/[sessionId]/page.tsx:12`).
Login redirect hardening rejects absolute and protocol-relative `next` values before returning `redirectTo` (`src/app/login/actions.ts:8`).
Invite-only signup is enforced server-side because `signup()` always returns an error, not only by hiding UI (`src/app/login/actions.ts:35`).
The proxy matcher stays scoped to `/admin/:path*`, `/host/:path*`, and `/login`, preserving public display/player bypass (`src/proxy.ts:8`).

findings.description: RLS appears to allow any authenticated host/admin to update any `game_states` row directly, bypassing server actions that enforce controller lock, call delay, and validation. Exploit scenario: a logged-in host uses the browser Supabase client to set `called_numbers`, `status`, or display fields for a game they do not control.
findings.evidence: `docs/schema.sql` defines `Hosts/Admins can update game state` with only a role existence check, and `Hosts can update sessions` likewise checks only host role (`docs/schema.sql:74`, `docs/schema.sql:128`).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The docs schema may not exactly match applied production RLS or table grants.
findings.what_would_confirm: Check live Supabase policies/grants or attempt a direct host-role update outside server actions.

findings.description: The started-game prize/structure lock is a read-then-write race. Failure scenario: a host starts a game after the admin action reads `game_states.status` but before the later `games.update`, allowing prizes, type, or stages to change after start.
findings.evidence: `updateGame` reads `game_states.status` into `isLocked`, then later updates `games` by `id` with no status predicate, lock, or transactional boundary (`src/app/admin/sessions/[id]/actions.ts:145`, `src/app/admin/sessions/[id]/actions.ts:223`).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The race needs concurrent admin and host actions, so it may not appear in manual single-user testing.
findings.what_would_confirm: Run a race/failure-injection test or move the lock check and update into one transactional RPC.

findings.description: Session delete protection is also a TOCTOU check outside the delete. Failure scenario: a host starts a ready game or records a winner after the pre-checks but before the session delete, and cascade deletion can erase newly live state or audit rows.
findings.evidence: `deleteSession` checks started states and winner count first, then performs a separate `.from('sessions').delete().eq('id', sessionId)` (`src/app/admin/actions.ts:117`, `src/app/admin/actions.ts:160`).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The window is small and requires concurrent host/admin actions.
findings.what_would_confirm: Use a transactional delete RPC with row locks or a conditional delete test that races game start/winner insert against deletion.

findings.description: `resetSession` performs destructive cleanup as separate writes, so partial failure can corrupt live history. Failure scenario: an admin resets during a live event, `game_states` deletion succeeds, then winner deletion or status update fails, leaving calls wiped but audit/session state inconsistent.
findings.evidence: The visible reset flow deletes `game_states` and then separately deletes `winners`, with independent error handling rather than a single transaction (`src/app/admin/sessions/[id]/actions.ts:397`, `src/app/admin/sessions/[id]/actions.ts:412`).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The truncated remainder could contain compensating logic, but it cannot make already-issued separate deletes atomic.
findings.what_would_confirm: Inspect or test the full reset path with an injected failure after `game_states` deletion.

findings.description: Snowball pot mutation is non-atomic and ignores several critical query/write errors, so jackpot money and history can drift. Failure scenario: a count or history insert fails, or two winner records race, causing a rollover/reset to be skipped, doubled, or applied without an audit row.
findings.evidence: `handleSnowballPotUpdate` reads winner count and pot data, updates `snowball_pots`, then inserts `snowball_pot_history` separately while ignoring some errors (`src/app/host/actions.ts:105`, `src/app/host/actions.ts:116`, `src/app/host/actions.ts:139`, `src/app/host/actions.ts:153`).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: A caller outside the visible snippet might serialize winner recording, but this helper itself has no transaction, idempotency key, or checked history write.
findings.what_would_confirm: Review `recordWinner` for a transactional RPC or add a concurrency test with repeated snowball winner submissions.

findings.description: The documented `profiles` RLS exposes staff emails and roles to public readers if applied in production. Exploit scenario: an anonymous Supabase client enumerates `profiles` to identify staff accounts and admin users.
findings.evidence: `docs/schema.sql` stores `profiles.email` and `profiles.role`, then creates `Public profiles are viewable by everyone` with `using (true)` (`docs/schema.sql:12`, `docs/schema.sql:21`).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: This may be legacy documentation rather than the applied production policy set.
findings.what_would_confirm: Check live `public.profiles` RLS policies and verify whether anon can select `email` and `role`.

findings.description: The display QR origin trusts forwarded host/proto headers without an allowlist. Exploit scenario: if the deployment proxy passes spoofed `x-forwarded-host`, the pub screen can show a valid-looking QR that sends guests to an attacker-controlled phishing domain.
findings.evidence: `playerJoinUrl` is built from `x-forwarded-host` or `host`, and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` is only used when no host exists (`src/app/display/[sessionId]/page.tsx:41`, `src/app/display/[sessionId]/page.tsx:48`).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Some hosts, such as Vercel behind trusted routing, may sanitize these headers before the app sees them.
findings.what_would_confirm: Verify the production proxy strips untrusted forwarded hosts, or add an allowed-origin check around QR URL construction.

empty_categories: No missing `getUser()`/role re-checks found in the changed server actions visible in the pack.
No public select list in the changed display/player pages includes `number_sequence`.
No raw SQL string interpolation, shell command construction, or `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` sink found in the changed TypeScript shown.
No hardcoded secrets or service-role key literals found in the changed files.
No unique constraint on `winners` was introduced in the included migrations, so multi-winner support is not blocked by the changed schema shown.
